Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Interface ICard

Namespace
Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces
Assembly
Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll

Represents an Apple II peripheral card that can be installed in expansion slots 1-7.

public interface ICard : IConfigurable
Inherited Members
IConfigurable.GetMetadata()
IConfigurable.ApplyMetadata(string)

Remarks

The Apple IIe provides seven expansion slots, each offering three distinct address spaces that cards can use for I/O operations, firmware, and extended functionality:

  • Card I/O ($C0x0-$C0xF): 16 bytes for hardware control registers
  • Card ROM ($Cx00-$CxFF): 256 bytes for boot/driver firmware
  • Extended ROM ($C800-$CFFF): 2KB shared space for extended firmware

All read methods return nullable bytes (Nullable<T>). A null return indicates the card did not respond at that address, allowing the system to return floating bus values or fall back to system ROM.

Each card type must implement the Clone() method to support multiple instances being created from a single registered prototype in the ICardFactory.

Configuration Management:
Cards implement IConfigurable, enabling save/restore of card-specific configuration (drive images, serial port settings, etc.). The metadata format is card-specific, but cards with child components (like drives) should use the hierarchical configuration pattern, embedding child metadata within their own. See IConfigurable for detailed guidance on configuration strategies.

Properties

Description

Gets a detailed description of the card's functionality and features.

string Description { get; }

Property Value

string

Remarks

This description can be displayed in tooltips, help text, or card selection dialogs to help users understand what the card does.

Id

Gets the unique numeric identifier for this card type.

int Id { get; }

Property Value

int

Remarks

While implemented as an instance property, this should return a constant value for all instances of the same card type. Each card implementation should use a unique ID (e.g., NullCard = 0, Disk II = 1, Serial = 2).

The ICardFactory validates that no two card types share the same ID during registration and will throw an exception if duplicates are detected.

Name

Gets the human-readable name of the card (e.g., "Disk II Controller", "Super Serial Card").

string Name { get; }

Property Value

string

Remarks

This name is used for display in UI and configuration. Card names should be unique across all card types to prevent ambiguity during card selection.

Methods

Clone()

Creates a new independent copy of this card instance.

ICard Clone()

Returns

ICard

A new ICard instance with the same type and configuration as this card.

Remarks

The ICardFactory maintains a single prototype instance of each card type. When a card is requested (via GetCardWithId(int) or GetCardWithName(string)), the factory calls Clone() to create a fresh, independent instance for the requesting slot.

This ensures that:

  • Each slot gets its own card instance with independent state
  • Multiple slots can use the same card type without interference
  • Removing a card from one slot doesn't affect cards in other slots

Implementation should typically return a new instance via constructor: 

public ICard Clone() => new MyCard();
If the card has configurable state, that state may need to be copied to the clone depending on the desired behavior.

ReadExtendedRom(ushort)

Reads a byte from the card's extended ROM address space ($C800-$CFFF).

byte? ReadExtendedRom(ushort offset)

Parameters

offset ushort

The offset within the 2KB extended ROM space (valid range: 0x0000-0x07FF / 0-2047).

Returns

byte?

The ROM byte if the card provides extended firmware at this address, or null if the card does not implement extended ROM. A null return allows the system to return floating bus values.

Remarks

The extended ROM space ($C800-$CFFF) is a 2KB region shared among all slots via bank switching. Only one card's extended ROM can be active at a time. The active card is determined by which slot last accessed address $CFFF.

Extended ROM is typically used for:

  • Additional driver code that doesn't fit in the 256-byte card ROM
  • Enhanced features and utilities
  • Configuration and diagnostic code

The ISlots implementation manages the bank selection mechanism, so individual cards only need to return their ROM data when queried.

ReadIO(byte)

Reads a byte from the card's I/O address space ($C0x0-$C0xF).

byte? ReadIO(byte offset)

Parameters

offset byte

The offset within the 16-byte I/O space (valid range: 0x00-0x0F).

Returns

byte?

The byte value if the card responds at this address, or null if the card does not implement this I/O address. A null return allows the system to return floating bus values.

Remarks

The I/O space maps to addresses $C0x0-$C0xF where 'x' represents the slot number plus 8 (slot 1 = $C090-$C09F, slot 2 = $C0A0-$C0AF, etc.). This space is typically used for hardware control registers, device status, and triggering operations.

Common usage patterns:

  • Reading device status flags
  • Receiving data from the peripheral
  • Triggering actions via read-triggered soft switches

ReadRom(byte)

Reads a byte from the card's ROM address space ($Cx00-$CxFF).

byte? ReadRom(byte offset)

Parameters

offset byte

The offset within the 256-byte ROM space (valid range: 0x00-0xFF).

Returns

byte?

The ROM byte if the card provides firmware at this address, or null if the card does not implement ROM or does not respond at this specific offset. A null return allows the system to fall back to internal ROM.

Remarks

The card ROM space maps to $Cx00-$CxFF where 'x' is the slot number (1-7). This space typically contains:

  • Boot ROM code that the system executes during slot scanning
  • Driver routines for the peripheral
  • Interface tables and vectors

The first few bytes ($Cx00-$Cx0F) are special:

  • $Cx01: Must be $20, $00, $03, or $3C to be recognized during boot scan
  • $Cx03, $Cx05, $Cx07: May contain entry point code

WriteExtendedRom(ushort, byte)

Writes a byte to the card's extended ROM address space ($C800-$CFFF).

void WriteExtendedRom(ushort offset, byte value)

Parameters

offset ushort

The offset within the 2KB extended ROM space (valid range: 0x0000-0x07FF / 0-2047).

value byte

The byte value to write.

Remarks

Like WriteRom(byte, byte), this is typically a no-op since extended ROM is usually read-only firmware. However, some cards may use this address range for writeable RAM or memory-mapped hardware.

If the card does not support writes to extended ROM space, this method should perform no operation rather than throwing an exception.

WriteIO(byte, byte)

Writes a byte to the card's I/O address space ($C0x0-$C0xF).

void WriteIO(byte offset, byte value)

Parameters

offset byte

The offset within the 16-byte I/O space (valid range: 0x00-0x0F).

value byte

The byte value to write to the card.

Remarks

The I/O space is used for sending commands and data to the peripheral device. Common operations include setting control flags, sending data bytes, and triggering hardware operations.

If the card does not implement a particular I/O address, this method should perform no operation (no-op) rather than throwing an exception.

WriteRom(byte, byte)

Writes a byte to the card's ROM address space ($Cx00-$CxFF).

void WriteRom(byte offset, byte value)

Parameters

offset byte

The offset within the 256-byte ROM space (valid range: 0x00-0xFF).

value byte

The byte value to write.

Remarks

In most cases, this is a no-op since card ROM is typically read-only firmware. However, some specialized cards may use this address space for writeable RAM or configuration registers.

If the card does not support writes to ROM space, this method should perform no operation rather than throwing an exception.

See Also

IConfigurable
ICardFactory
ISlots
